one_line_takeaway: A descriptive taxonomy study showing that 100 GPTZero-flagged fabricated citations in 53 NeurIPS 2025 papers are dominated by wholesale Total Fabrication (66%) and universally exhibit compound (multi-mechanism) failure, arguing that peer review lacks any systematic citation-verification step and should mandate automated multi-attribute checking.
key_finding: All 100 hallucinated citations (100%) exhibited compound failure modes, i.e. more than one deception mechanism simultaneously. Primary modes were dominated by Total Fabrication (66%) and Partial Attribute Corruption (27%); the most common secondary characteristics were Semantic Hallucination (63% of all citations) and Identifier Hijacking (29%). Notably 50 of the 66 Total Fabrications (76%) layered a domain-appropriate, plausible-sounding title (SH) on top of wholesale invention. The author argues this multi-layered structure — plausible titles, familiar author names, and working-but-mismatched identifiers — is why 3-5 expert reviewers per paper failed to catch fabrications, and recommends mandatory four-step automated citation verification at submission.
core_contribution: A single-author, descriptive content-analysis study that applies a newly proposed five-category failure-mode taxonomy to 100 AI-generated hallucinated citations found in 53 accepted NeurIPS 2025 papers. IMPORTANT PROVENANCE: the 100-citation dataset itself was NOT collected by this paper — it comes from GPTZero's public report (ref [1]), whose automated Hallucination Check tool scanned 4,841 of the 5,290 accepted papers and whose human experts verified each flag. The author's OWN contributions are: (1) the five-category taxonomy (Total Fabrication, Partial Attribute Corruption, Identifier Hijacking, Semantic Hallucination, Placeholder Hallucination), (2) manual dual-coding (primary + secondary failure mode) of all 100 citations, (3) the empirical 'compound failure' finding that 100% of citations exhibit more than one failure mode, and (4) a proposed sixth mechanism, 'Contamination Inheritance' (CI), where an LLM reproduces a pre-existing erroneous citation inherited from polluted training data rather than fabricating de novo (identified in >=1 case, the Z. Zhu et al. citation traced to a since-corrected arXiv v1).
threat_model: Not a security paper and no deliberate adversary is modeled. The paper explicitly disclaims the ability to determine author intent (fraud vs. negligent AI use vs. honest mistake) and focuses on the observable failure (citations are fabricated) plus the systemic gap (peer review does not verify citations). The closest adversarial-adjacent notions are (a) fabricated citations that 'exploit' reviewer verification heuristics superficially, and (b) 'Contamination Inheritance' as a form of training-data pollution feedback loop — but these are framed as emergent statistical artifacts of LLM next-token prediction, not as an attacker's capability. It belongs to research-integrity / trustworthy-AI rather than AI security; imposing a formal threat model would misrepresent the paper.
limitations: Provenance/credit risk: the dataset is GPTZero's, not the author's, so findings are bounded by GPTZero's tool recall — only automatically-flagged citations were analyzed, missing citations that are real but subtly misrepresent source content. Single-coder design with no inter-rater reliability, making the taxonomy assignments subjective (the author concedes PAC-vs-TF boundaries are ambiguous). The headline '100% compound failure rate' is partly an artifact of a coding scheme that mandates assigning a secondary code to every citation, so it should be read cautiously rather than as evidence of deliberate multi-layered design. Data is a single conference in a single discipline (NeurIPS 2025, AI/ML), so the 66% TF / 27% PAC distribution is explicitly non-generalizable across venues, LLMs, or fields. Author intent is undeterminable. Several corroborating cases (ICLR, MAHA report, Deloitte) are cited from journalistic/vendor sources rather than independently analyzed.

datasets_used: GPTZero NeurIPS 2025 Hallucination Check public report (ref [1]): 100 verified hallucinated citations across 53 accepted papers; tool scanned 4,841 of 5,290 accepted NeurIPS 2025 papers
Contextual figures: NeurIPS 2025 had 21,575 submissions and 5,290 accepted papers (24.52% acceptance rate); 53 contaminated papers ~= 1% of accepted
Referenced (not analyzed) corroborating cases: GPTZero ICLR 2026 report (>50 hallucinations, some rated 8/10); MAHA government report; Deloitte Australia (AUD $98,000 refund)
quantitative_results: Primary failure-mode distribution: Total Fabrication 66% (66), Partial Attribute Corruption 27% (27), Identifier Hijacking 4% (4), Placeholder Hallucination 2% (2), Semantic Hallucination 1% (1)
Secondary failure characteristics: Semantic Hallucination 63% (63), Identifier Hijacking 29% (29), Placeholder Hallucination 4% (4), Partial Attribute Corruption 3% (3), Total Fabrication 1% (1)
100% of the 100 citations exhibited compound (>=2) failure modes
50 of 66 Total Fabrications (76%) used Semantic Hallucination as their secondary mode

Per-paper counts across 53 papers: mean 1.89, median 2, range 1-13; bimodal — 49/53 papers (92%) had 1-2 hallucinations, 4 outliers had 4-13; max single paper had 13 ('Efficient semantic uncertainty quantification in language models via diversity-steered sampling')
Contamination Inheritance (CI) newly named mechanism, identified in >=1 case (Z. Zhu et al. NeuralRGB-D citation traced to Beltran et al. 2024 arXiv:2412.13176v1)
baselines_compared: None — descriptive/frequency taxonomy study with no comparative baseline or competing method; GPTZero's tool is the data source rather than a compared system
